author: @madmaveric
text:
Nice project. Sometimes a cheap start (buying two cheap bikes) is worth the time saved trying to match up two different but free bikes :D 
Will be interesting to see how the wheels hold up (being kids wheels), they will hold the weight ok but the sideways force (something they are not really designed for anyway) might be an issue. I guess it depends on the speed of the motor you use. I'm a fan of try it and see as well though (although I don't bounce like I did when I was a kid lol)

From my days of carts made from old prams as a kid 40 years ago (the type made from a couple of planks of wood and string/feet for steering) I found that out a few times the hard way. Turning at faster than walking pace risked the wheels buckling. No helmets back then either, just a Mom to kiss it better and send us back outside out of the way while she finished cooking/cleaning (code for watching her soaps on TV :) ) lol I think I learned by my third or fourth one that the wheels were never meant to be hurled down big grass slopes with no brakes and a fast turn at the bottom :D

author: @AndreaDingbatt
text:
Oh Wow!! Thank you so much for the upload!!
I will be watching this build with great interest!

I managed to buy a 2nd hand E-bike, for under £200!!
But I need to get a sidecar welded on, for my support /assist dog Jasper!

And also, due to someone purposely puncturing the front tyre, I need to find someone who can fix this, I don't have the bike pump, or any tools to fix this with! 😔
As well as being too disabled to do the work.. 

I now keep the E-bike in my bedsit, so the back tyre is left alone!!

I bought the bike because, on it I am just another cyclist, whereas, on the mobility scooter, I was treated as a disabled person....
There is a Massive difference.

Andrea, Jasper and George the Pigeon. XxX.

author: @davidsliwinski5834
text: Robert, you maniac!!! You've done it again. Over the years I have had various ideas pop into my mind and they never go anywhere as I am a "Theoretical Inventor". You actually turn ideas into three-dimensional reality. Bravo! Ideas don't mean much if not turned into actual objects. Can't wait to see what you produce next!

author: @mulgerbill
text:
Rear axles may be an issue, with those screw on freewheel clusters there's a fair length of unsupported axle from the right face of the hub to the dropouts. Having two should help spread the load but I'd be keeping a close eye on them for the first few shakedown runs. It wouldn't hurt to read up on wheel truing and check your spoke tension, I've never come across a factory built wheelset that was really tight and evenly tensioned out of the box.
Quibbles aside, the steering looks to be a bit of genius. I'm looking forward to watching this project mature

author: @robertpitt8418
text: Wow that looks absolutely brilliant, looking forward to the follow up on this project

author: @Barskor1
text:
Nice project Robert, Mountain bikes with front shock absorber forks are a really good idea.
Steering is easy you take the front handlebars refix them 90 degrees from normal position chop off one end drill a hole and attach a bar connecting the two.
For the back, I chopped it so the crankshafts were pivot points and put  ATV shock absorbers between them and a  DIY frame, with hub motors you can have all-wheel drive and go crazy fast added a roll cage for that reason.
